summary: Skeletal muscle fibres are multinucleated with peripherally placed nuclei and prominent cross-striations.
Skeletal muscle fibres have a single centrally placed nucleus.
Cardiac muscle has centrally placed nuclei, cross-striations, and intercalated discs containing gap junctions for electrical coupling.
Cardiac muscle has peripherally placed nuclei like skeletal muscle.
Cardiac muscle lacks gap junctions between adjacent cells.
Smooth muscle cells are spindle-shaped with a single central nucleus, lack striations, and are innervated by the autonomic nervous system.
Smooth muscle cells are cylindrical with multiple nuclei.
Smooth muscle is under voluntary control via somatic nerves.
The sarcomere is the basic contractile unit of striated muscle, bounded by Z lines on either side.
The sarcomere is bounded by M lines on either side.
The sarcomere is the contractile unit unique to smooth muscle.
The A band contains thick myosin filaments and appears dark, while the I band contains only thin actin filaments and appears light.
The I band contains myosin filaments and appears dark.
Both A and I bands contain identical proportions of actin and myosin.
Intercalated discs are unique to cardiac muscle and contain desmosomes for mechanical coupling and gap junctions for electrical coupling.
Intercalated discs are found in both skeletal and cardiac muscle.
Intercalated discs contain tight junctions but not gap junctions.
